findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py executes a script file via importlib at import time, injecting it into sys.modules as a side effect
evidence: Lines 118-123: importlib.util.spec_from_file_location, module_from_spec, and exec_module are called unconditionally at module import time, loading 06_llm_review.py and registering it as scripts.llm_review in sys.modules
Line 122: sys.modules[_module_name] = _llm_review — global interpreter state mutated at import time
Lines 126-127: main = _llm_review.main and ReviewMode = _llm_review.ReviewMode bind names from the dynamically loaded module; any import error in 06_llm_review.py silently corrupts scripts/__init__

06_llm_review.py is an entry-point script, not a library module; treating it as an importable library by loading it from __init__.py blurs the boundary between orchestration scripts and infrastructure modules
findings.suggestion: Move ReviewMode and the re-exported LLM functions into an infrastructure module (e.g., infrastructure/llm/review/modes.py). Then scripts/__init__.py can import them with a normal static import, eliminating the dynamic exec and sys.modules mutation.

findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py re-exports 25+ symbols from infrastructure modules, turning the scripts package into a second public API surface for infrastructure
evidence: Lines 92-114: imports generate_executive_summary, generate_quality_review, generate_methodology_review, generate_improvement_suggestions, validate_review_quality, save_review_outputs, generate_review_summary, ReviewMetrics, ManuscriptMetrics, SessionMetrics, estimate_tokens, get_max_input_length, check_ollama_availability, extract_manuscript_text all from infrastructure subpackages
Lines 130-133: re-exports is_off_topic, detect_conversational_phrases, check_format_compliance from infrastructure.llm.validation.format
Lines 138-146: re-exports LLMClient, LLMConfig, is_ollama_running, select_best_model from infrastructure.llm — all wrapped in a silent except ImportError: pass
__all__ at lines 156-182 lists 26 symbols, most of which live in infrastructure — callers who import from scripts are coupled to infrastructure internals through this facade
findings.suggestion: Remove the re-export surface from scripts/__init__.py. Tests that need LLM infrastructure utilities should import directly from infrastructure.llm.*. scripts/__init__.py should be empty or limited to MENU_SCRIPT_MAPPING.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/file_operations.py uses an inline directory scan to avoid importing infrastructure.project.discovery, documenting a hidden circular dependency risk
evidence: Lines 114-122: comment reads 'Using Path scan instead of infrastructure.project.discovery avoids a circular import: file_operations -> project.discovery -> core.logging_utils'

The workaround is functional but documents that the dependency direction between core/ and project/ is bidirectional; project.discovery imports from core (logging_utils), while core would naturally want to call project.discovery
This is a structural signal that project-level discovery logic is not fully separated from core utilities
findings.suggestion: Extract the 'list valid project names' capability into a standalone utility that neither core/ nor project/ depends on circularly, or ensure project.discovery is the sole entry point and core modules receive project names as arguments rather than discovering them themselves.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/integrity.py re-exports calculate_file_hash from infrastructure.core.file_operations with a noqa:F401 comment, creating an unintentional second public import path
evidence: Line 23: from infrastructure.core.file_operations import calculate_file_hash  # noqa: F401

The noqa comment suppresses the 'imported but unused' warning; the symbol is re-exported for callers who import it from validation.integrity rather than from its canonical home in core.file_operations
Grep shows only 3 callers of the integrity module and none import calculate_file_hash through it; the re-export appears to be legacy remnant
findings.suggestion: Remove the re-export line from integrity.py. Callers that need calculate_file_hash should import it directly from infrastructure.core.file_operations.

findings.summary: scripts/02_run_analysis.py omits log_header and the stage-open log_success pattern used by all numbered sibling scripts
evidence: Packet sibling_behavior for scripts/: log_header used in 13/18 scripts, log_success used in 11/18 scripts; scripts/02_run_analysis.py listed as outlier missing both
00_setup_environment.py line 22 imports log_success and log_header; 01_run_tests.py line 29 imports log_success and log_header — pattern is consistent across peer scripts
02_run_analysis.py imports log_success (line 37) but not log_header; it produces no stage-open banner analogous to what other numbered scripts emit at their entry point
findings.suggestion: Add a log_header call at the start of main() in 02_run_analysis.py, matching the pattern used in 00_, 01_, 03_, etc. Import log_header alongside log_success.

findings.summary: scripts/generate_test_summary.py is a 3-line delegation stub with no logger, no log_header, no log_success, and no annotations — structurally unlike all numbered pipeline scripts
evidence: File body (lines 1-18): imports only Path and sys; no get_logger, no log_header, no log_success, no type annotations; body is a two-line delegation to run_test_summary_generation()
Packet outliers list confirms all three patterns (get_logger, log_header, log_success) missing
Sibling scripts (00_, 01_, 02_, 03_) all set up a logger, emit a stage header, and log success/failure — this file does none of that, making pipeline log output inconsistent for this step
findings.suggestion: Add a main() wrapper with get_logger, a log_header call, and log_success/failure around run_test_summary_generation(), following the same structure as 01_run_tests.py.

findings.summary: scripts/execute_pipeline.py is the only numbered pipeline entry point missing from __future__ import annotations, inconsistent with all other scripts in the package
evidence: Packet outliers for scripts/: execute_pipeline.py listed as missing 'annotations'
All other 15/18 scripts in the directory include from __future__ import annotations as the first import; execute_pipeline.py is one of three outliers, and the only core pipeline executor among them
findings.suggestion: Add from __future__ import annotations at the top of scripts/execute_pipeline.py after the module docstring.

findings.summary: tests/ directory contains one camelCase identifier against a codebase that is uniformly snake_case
evidence: Packet naming_by_directory for 'tests/': snake_case=267, camelCase=1 — a single camelCase name exists where none is expected
All other directories show 0 camelCase occurrences; Python naming guidance in the packet explicitly states 'Check for Java-style camelCase leaking in'
findings.suggestion: Locate the camelCase identifier (likely a function or variable name in an infra_tests file) and rename it to snake_case.

findings.summary: Multiple modules in validation/ and rendering/ use bare except Exception: pass blocks that silently discard errors with no log, no propagation, and no recovery
evidence: repo_scanner.py lines 160-161, 220-221, 310-311, 366-367, 432-433: five separate except Exception: pass blocks; no logger.debug, no re-raise, no recovery action
doc_accuracy.py lines 238-239, 276-277, 294-295: three except Exception: pass blocks inside validation logic that scans documents for accuracy issues
doc_completeness.py lines 72-73: except Exception: pass inside a documentation completeness check loop
doc_discovery.py lines 134-135: except Exception: pass
pdf_renderer.py lines 701-702: except Exception: pass inside a content-reading fallback path
rendering/core.py lines 95-96: except Exception: pass with comment 'Ignore errors when checking PDF status'
latex_package_validator.py lines 83-84: except Exception: pass
The dominant pattern in the same codebase is except Exception as e: logger.error(..., exc_info=True) or raise, making the silent passes clear outliers
findings.suggestion: Replace bare except Exception: pass blocks with at minimum logger.debug('...', exc_info=True) so the error is observable under debug logging. Where the exception indicates a real failure rather than an optional probe, raise or propagate it.

findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py contains two silent exception swallows at import time: one bare except ImportError: pass and one bare except Exception: pass
evidence: Lines 144-146: except ImportError: pass wraps import of LLMClient, LLMConfig, is_ollama_running, select_best_model — if any of these fail for a reason other than the module being absent (e.g., a syntax error in the module), the error is discarded silently

Lines 152-154: except Exception: pass wraps from scripts.execute_pipeline import execute_pipeline, execute_single_stage and from scripts.execute_multi_project import execute_multi_project — any import-time exception in these core pipeline scripts is silently swallowed at package load time, leaving execute_pipeline, execute_single_stage, execute_multi_project undefined with no signal to the caller

Symbols listed in __all__ (lines 178-180) include execute_pipeline, execute_single_stage, execute_multi_project — if the silent except fires, these names will be absent from __all__ exports with no error at import time
findings.suggestion: For the ImportError block, add logger.debug(...) so import failures are visible. For the except Exception block around execute_pipeline imports, remove it entirely or replace with except ImportError — catching all exceptions at package load time can mask genuine bugs in core pipeline code.

findings.summary: The validation/ layer mixes custom TemplateError subclasses with bare stdlib exceptions, depending on which validator is called
evidence: pdf_validator.py raises PDFValidationError (a TemplateError subclass from infrastructure.core.exceptions) at lines 42, 51, 56, 106

markdown_validator.py raises FileNotFoundError and NotADirectoryError — these are the custom dual-inheritance exceptions from infrastructure.core.exceptions (line 19 confirms the import), so they ARE in the hierarchy, but the public raise signature looks like stdlib builtins to call-site readers

integrity.py at line 23 re-exports calculate_file_hash with noqa:F401 and itself never raises a custom exception — errors are returned as Dict[str, bool] False values (returns_null pattern per the packet)

Within the same validation/ package, errors are signalled via: (a) raised TemplateError subclasses (pdf_validator), (b) raised dual-inheritance builtins (markdown_validator), (c) returned bool/dict (integrity), and (d) silent except pass (repo_scanner, doc_accuracy) — four distinct strategies with no documented layering rationale
findings.suggestion: Establish a single error contract for the validation layer: raise TemplateError subclasses for unrecoverable failures and return structured results (e.g., a ValidationResult dataclass) for partial/soft failures. Remove the returns_null and silent-pass patterns from public validation functions.

findings.summary: LLMClient accepts a broad config dict with 201 config-bag mentions, mixing connection params, generation options, system prompt injection, streaming, context management, and retry policy in one object
evidence: review_packet: wide_param_bags entry for infrastructure/llm/core/client.py shows wide_functions=1 and config_bag_mentions=201, the highest in the entire codebase
LLMConfig dataclass (config.py) mixes connection URL, auth, timeout, retry, streaming, context-window, generation defaults, and system prompt injection into a single flat struct with no sub-grouping
client.py has chain_count=85 at max_chain_depth=3, indicating substantial indirection layering on top of an already-wide config surface
findings.suggestion: Split LLMConfig into at least two focused sub-configs (e.g. ConnectionConfig for URL/timeout/retry and GenerationDefaults for temperature/tokens/seed). Pass them as typed arguments rather than one omnibus object. This also reduces chain depth by removing intermediate dispatch logic.

findings.summary: Test files use named lambdas (obj_func, grad_func, test_func) that only forward to the real function without adding any policy, inflating the pass-through wrapper count to 14 in one file
evidence: review_packet pass_through_wrappers: test_optimizer.py count=14, samples include obj_func->quadratic_function and grad_func->compute_gradient repeated across every test
generate_api_docs.py count=4; optimization_analysis.py count=2 with test_func->quadratic_function
These wrappers add no retries, logging, or translation — they are pure forwarding aliases
findings.suggestion: Pass quadratic_function and compute_gradient directly as callables to the optimizer under test. Reserve named wrappers only when the test needs to intercept or alter behavior.

findings.summary: log_operation_silent is a one-line context manager that only calls log_operation with log_completion=False — it adds no policy beyond what the parameter already expresses
evidence: logging_utils.py lines 281-287: log_operation_silent is defined as 'with log_operation(operation, logger, level, log_completion=False): yield' — the full body is one delegation line
The docstring reads 'Thin wrapper over log_operation', confirming this is a recognized pass-through with no added behavior beyond renaming
findings.suggestion: Remove log_operation_silent and have callers pass log_completion=False directly to log_operation. If the pattern is frequent enough to justify a name, document the call-site count; otherwise the wrapper adds import surface with no benefit.

findings.summary: infrastructure/steganography/overlays.py contains 2 wide functions with 0 config-bag mentions, meaning broad positional parameter lists rather than a structured config
evidence: review_packet wide_param_bags: overlays.py wide_functions=2, config_bag_mentions=0 — the parameters are not packed into a config object, so each call site must pass each value independently
Sibling file steganography/core.py has wide_functions=1 but config_bag_mentions=37, showing the rest of the module does use config objects
findings.suggestion: Consolidate the wide parameter lists into the existing SteganographyConfigYAML or a dedicated OverlayConfig dataclass (already partially defined in infrastructure/core/config_loader.py) so call sites pass one typed object instead of a long positional sequence.

findings.summary: plotly is listed as an optional dependency-group ('dashboard') but dashboard_generator.py imports matplotlib at module level and lazily imports plotly, meaning the import boundary is not enforced by the package structure
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 63-65: plotly is in [dependency-groups] dashboard, declared optional
dashboard_generator.py lines 16-18: matplotlib and numpy are imported at module level; plotly is imported lazily inside functions
pyproject.toml dev group (line 89 comment) does NOT include plotly, so CI runs without it; any test that exercises a plotly code path will ImportError silently unless caught
findings.suggestion: Either add plotly to the dev dependency group so test runs are consistent, or add an explicit 'pytest.importorskip("plotly")' guard at the top of dashboard_generator tests. The current state creates a latent CI failure gap.

findings.summary: psutil is listed as a required core dependency in pyproject.toml but it is only used for optional resource-usage logging, with a try/except ImportError fallback in the consuming function
evidence: pyproject.toml line 43: 'psutil>=5.9.0' in [project] dependencies — this is a hard install-time requirement
logging_utils.py lines 523-546: log_resource_usage() wraps all psutil access in try/except ImportError and logs a debug message if unavailable — the code gracefully handles psutil absence
This means every consumer of the package must install psutil even though the only use is a best-effort debug helper
findings.suggestion: Move psutil from [project] dependencies to a [dependency-groups] monitoring optional group (similar to how ollama and dotenv are handled). The existing ImportError fallback already handles the absent case correctly.

findings.summary: tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py uses unittest.mock.patch to simulate network errors, directly contradicting the project's 'No Mocks' policy documented in CLAUDE.md and pyproject.toml comments
evidence: test_logging.py line 375: 'from unittest.mock import patch'
test_logging.py line 387: 'with patch("infrastructure.llm.core.client.requests.post", side_effect=requests.exceptions.ConnectionError(...))' — patches an internal implementation detail
test_logging.py line 398-410: second patch block for Timeout, same pattern
CLAUDE.md states: 'ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT: Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock, or any mocking framework. HTTP testing: Use pytest-httpserver for local test servers'
pytest-httpserver is already available (pyproject.toml line 80) and used in the ollama_test_server fixture in the same test file — so the compliant pattern exists but is not used for error path tests
findings.suggestion: Replace the two patch() blocks with pytest-httpserver fixtures that respond with 500-level or connection-refused behavior. The existing ollama_test_server fixture in the conftest already demonstrates the correct pattern for this test file.

findings.summary: projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py has 3 importers but zero direct test coverage per the critical_untested list in the review packet
evidence: review_packet testing.critical_untested: [{"file": "projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py", "importers": 3}]
styles.py defines CASE_COLORS, WIRE_COLORS, DiagramTheme, get_case_color, get_wire_color — all pure data/logic with no I/O dependencies

The three test files that import from visualization.styles do so incidentally (e.g. test_diagram_generator.py imports CASE_COLORS at line 34 only to assert a color value inside another test class), not as dedicated coverage for styles.py contract
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated TestStyles class in an existing test file (test_edge_cases.py already imports get_case_color and get_wire_color) that verifies: (1) all CaseRole members appear as keys in CASE_COLORS, (2) get_case_color returns the correct hex for each role, (3) get_wire_color handles unknown tokens with the default color, (4) DiagramTheme enum values are the expected strings.

findings.summary: The pipeline's stage-execution and checkpoint-resume logic is tested only at the unit level (test_pipeline.py, test_checkpoint.py); there are no cross-module integration tests verifying that stages hand off correctly through the real pipeline coordinator
evidence: review_packet testing.total_files=360 but testing.critical_untested lists only styles.py — however the packet's indirection_hotspots show infrastructure/core/pipeline.py has chain_count=50 at depth 3, indicating substantial orchestration logic
execute_pipeline.py is the real pipeline entry point but has no corresponding test file in tests/infra_tests/

test_pipeline.py (14 test functions) and test_checkpoint.py (19 test functions) test units in isolation, but no test exercises resume-from-checkpoint across actual stage boundaries with the real subprocess execution path
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test (marked @pytest.mark.integration) that runs execute_pipeline.py as a subprocess against a minimal project fixture, verifies at least two stages complete and write the expected checkpoint file, then resumes and verifies the remaining stages run. This would catch coordinator-level regressions not visible in unit tests.

findings.summary: LogAggregator class and collect_log_statistics function live inside logging_utils.py but are never imported anywhere else in the codebase
evidence: LogAggregator class defined at line 597 of logging_utils.py with full add_message, get_summary, generate_report, and save_to_file methods
collect_log_statistics function defined at line 698 of logging_utils.py
Grep for 'LogAggregator' across all .py files returns exactly one match: its own definition site
Grep for 'collect_log_statistics' across all .py files returns exactly one match: its own definition site
Neither name appears in logging_utils __all__ export list (lines 551-589)
findings.suggestion: Delete LogAggregator and collect_log_statistics from logging_utils.py. If they serve a real purpose, move them to a dedicated infrastructure/reporting/log_aggregator.py that is actually imported by callers.

findings.summary: log_operation_silent is a one-line thin wrapper over log_operation with no callers outside its own file
evidence: log_operation_silent defined at lines 281-287: 'with log_operation(operation, logger, level, log_completion=False): yield' — adds nothing except renaming one keyword argument
Grep for 'log_operation_silent' across all .py files returns only the definition file (infrastructure/core/logging_utils.py)
It appears in __all__ (line 559 region) but no importer pulls it
findings.suggestion: Remove log_operation_silent. Callers can pass log_completion=False to log_operation directly. If retained for API stability, document that it exists only as a convenience alias.

findings.summary: Trivial utility functions carry multi-line docstrings with Args/Returns sections that restate exactly what the signature already says
evidence: log_success (lines 361-375): 4-line docstring on a 2-line function body; Args section says 'message: Success message' which adds no information beyond the parameter name
log_header (lines 378-401): 5-line docstring followed by body that calls logger.info four times; Returns nothing, side-effect-only — docstring Example block duplicates the code
check_python_version (lines 50-65 of environment.py): docstring says 'Returns: True if Python version is 3.8 or higher, False otherwise' — the function body is 8 lines, the docstring is 5
check_build_tools (lines 206-234 of environment.py): identical pattern — docstring restates arg types and return semantics already visible from the signature
findings.suggestion: Collapse restating docstrings on sub-10-line utilities to a single summary line. Reserve Args/Returns sections for functions where the parameter contract is genuinely non-obvious.

findings.summary: Infrastructure functions emit logger.info on every entry ('Checking Python version...', 'Checking dependencies...', 'Setting up directory structure...') without diagnostic value
evidence: check_python_version line 56: logger.info('Checking Python version...') — single-line check that takes <1ms
check_dependencies line 79: logger.info('Checking dependencies...') before a for-loop over 4 packages
check_build_tools line 216: logger.info('Checking build tools...')
setup_directories line 283: logger.info('Setting up directory structure...')
clean_output_directory line 33: logger.info('Cleaning output directory...')
Pattern is copy-pasted verbatim across all five environment-check helpers with no additional context
findings.suggestion: Demote entry announcements to logger.debug. Reserve logger.info for results ('Python 3.11.2 OK', 'pandoc missing'). The current pattern produces redundant noise in normal pipeline runs.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains two near-identical performance modules: performance.py and performance_monitor.py, both defining CodeProfiler, benchmark_function, and benchmark_llm_query
evidence: performance.py defines: PerformanceMonitor (line 73), CodeProfiler (line 365), benchmark_function (line 567), benchmark_llm_query (line 539)
performance_monitor.py defines: CodeProfiler (line 60), benchmark_function (line 362), benchmark_llm_query (line 293) — same class names, same method signatures
infrastructure/core/__init__.py imports exclusively from performance.py (lines 92-106), so performance_monitor.py exports are dead at the package boundary
infrastructure/core/ has 27 files (directory_profiles shows file_count: 27); performance_monitor.py is a leftover split artifact that was never cleaned up
findings.suggestion: Delete performance_monitor.py. All callers already import from performance.py via core/__init__.py. Verify no direct imports of performance_monitor remain before deletion.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains two files — summary_generator.py and test_summary_generator.py — with identical load_test_results and load_infrastructure_results implementations
evidence: summary_generator.py lines 20-45: load_test_results with identical body to test_summary_generator.py lines 20-45

summary_generator.py lines 48-89: load_infrastructure_results with identical fallback logic, same JSON key paths, same hardcoded file paths ('infrastructure_validation_report.json', 'coverage_infra.json')
Both files import the same dependencies (json, datetime, Path, get_testing_config, get_logger)
reporting/__init__.py does not export either module's load_* functions, suggesting both files are called directly by scripts rather than through the package interface
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single infrastructure/reporting/test_results_loader.py. Export load_test_results and load_infrastructure_results from reporting/__init__.py. Remove whichever duplicate file has fewer callers.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 27 files mixing six distinct concerns with no sub-grouping, making ownership boundaries and change scope unpredictable
evidence: directory_profiles shows infrastructure/core/ file_count: 27, total_loc: 9616

Files span six distinct concerns: logging (logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py — 5 files), performance (performance.py, performance_monitor.py — 2 files, one duplicate), pipeline execution (pipeline.py, pipeline_summary.py, checkpoint.py, multi_project.py), security (security.py, credentials.py), environment/setup (environment.py, script_discovery.py, health_check.py), and utilities (menu.py, retry.py, progress.py, file_operations.py, file_inventory.py, errors.py, exceptions.py, config_loader.py, config_cli.py, cli.py)
avg_fan_in of 9.6 means every file in core/ is imported by ~10 other files on average — any rename or split creates widespread ripple edits
Security and health check modules have very different update cadence from config/logging utilities but live at the same directory depth
findings.suggestion: Extract infrastructure/core/logging/ (5 logging_* files), infrastructure/core/perf/ (performance.py after deleting performance_monitor.py), and infrastructure/core/security/ (security.py, credentials.py) as sub-packages. Keep config_loader, exceptions, errors, environment, pipeline, and multi_project in core/ where their fan-in justifies top-level placement.

findings.summary: SecurityValidator, RateLimiter, SecurityHeaders, and SecurityMonitor live in infrastructure/core/security.py, a concern orthogonal to the 'foundation utilities' role that core/ documents for itself
evidence: infrastructure/core/__init__.py module docstring (lines 1-10): 'Foundation utilities — configuration management, logging, and exception hierarchy'
security.py implements SecurityValidator with 14 dangerous-pattern regexes, a threading-based RateLimiter, SecurityHeaders dataclass, and SecurityMonitor — none of which are 'foundation utilities'

infrastructure/__init__.py module docstring does not list security as a top-level infrastructure sub-module (it lists core, validation, documentation, scientific, llm, rendering, publishing, reporting, steganography)

security.py is consumed primarily by LLM input validation (validate_llm_input is exported from core/__init__.py line 127) — its natural home is infrastructure/llm/ or a dedicated infrastructure/security/ package
findings.suggestion: Move security.py (and credentials.py) to infrastructure/security/ as a first-class sub-package. Update core/__init__.py to re-export the handful of functions used by LLM code. This makes core/ match its stated role and gives security concerns a navigable home.

findings.summary: Multi-project orchestration responsibility is split between infrastructure/core/multi_project.py and scripts/execute_multi_project.py with no clear ownership boundary documented
evidence: infrastructure/core/multi_project.py defines MultiProjectOrchestrator with execute_all_projects_full, execute_all_projects_core methods — business logic
scripts/execute_multi_project.py is described in CLAUDE.md as the entry point for multi-project runs
multi_project.py lives in core/ (27-file directory) even though it orchestrates project-level pipelines — a level above what 'core foundation utilities' implies

The two-layer architecture documented in CLAUDE.md places scripts as 'thin orchestrators' but multi_project.py in core/ is itself a thick orchestrator importing from pipeline.py, errors.py, and project/discovery.py
findings.suggestion: Move MultiProjectOrchestrator to infrastructure/project/ (which already has discovery.py and a clear project-management mandate). This aligns ownership: infrastructure/project/ owns all project-lifecycle logic, core/ owns foundation utilities.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains 15 files mixing four distinct sub-concerns (test reporting, executive summaries, dashboards, HTML templates) with no internal grouping, making the module's public contract unclear
evidence: directory_profiles shows infrastructure/reporting/ file_count: 15, total_loc: 9255

reporting/__init__.py exports 20+ names spanning generate_pipeline_report, generate_coverage_test_report, generate_executive_summary, generate_all_dashboards, OutputOrganizer — four functionally distinct concerns
test_reporter.py and coverage_reporter.py both parse pytest JSON output; their split rationale is not documented
generate_multi_project_summary_report is defined in pipeline_reporter.py (lines 36-80+) even though it is a multi-project concern, not a single-pipeline concern
html_templates.py is a data-only module (string literals) co-located with algorithmic report generators — no sub-directory separates static assets from logic
findings.suggestion: Split reporting/ into reporting/pipeline/ (pipeline_reporter, error_aggregator, output_organizer), reporting/test/ (test_reporter, coverage_reporter, test_summary_generator, summary_generator merged), and reporting/executive/ (executive_reporter, multi_project_reporter, dashboard_generator). This gives each sub-concern a predictable home and shrinks __init__.py's re-export surface.

findings.summary: Pipeline stage list uses raw tuple indexing instead of a typed protocol, forcing every consumer to know the implicit (name, func[, skip]) arity
evidence: Line 183-186: `stage_name = stage_spec[0]; stage_func = stage_spec[1]; skip_condition = stage_spec[2] if len(stage_spec) > 2 else False` — positional indexing repeated in both `_execute_pipeline` and `_resume_pipeline`
Line 334-365 in `_resume_pipeline` repeats the same three-position unpack logic verbatim, including the `if len(stage_spec) > 2` guard
The tuple layout is an implicit contract not enforced by any type — adding a fourth element would silently pass and be ignored
findings.suggestion: Replace the raw tuple with a `@dataclass` or `NamedTuple` (e.g. `PipelineStage(name, func, skip=False)`) and extract the iteration + skip guard into a single helper so both `_execute_pipeline` and `_resume_pipeline` share one code path.

findings.summary: Review type dispatch in `run_llm_review_pipeline` is an explicit if/elif chain over string literals rather than a mapping, leaking the dispatch contract across two modules
evidence: pipeline_runner.py lines 137-147: four `if/elif` branches on `review_type` string to call the corresponding `generate_*` function — the same string literals appear in `generator.py` `validate_review_quality` (lines 161, 189, 223, 253, 283)
Adding a new review type requires changes in at least three places: the if/elif dispatcher, the validator, and the `REVIEW_MIN_WORDS` dict in `manuscript.py`
The `ReviewMode` class uses bare string constants (line 42-44) with no link to the per-type generator mapping, adding a third encoding of the same concept
findings.suggestion: Define a `REVIEW_GENERATORS: dict[str, Callable]` mapping at the module boundary in `generator.py` and replace the if/elif chain with a single lookup. The validator can reference the same dict to enumerate known types.

findings.summary: The partial-response-save block inside `stream_query` is copy-pasted verbatim into four separate exception branches, creating 4x maintenance surface for one piece of boundary logic
evidence: Lines 947-973 (Timeout retry path): constructs `ResponseMetadata`, calls `save_streaming_response`, catches `Exception as save_error`
Lines 978-1004 (Timeout final failure path): identical block, only `partial_response=True` and `streaming_time_seconds` differ
Lines 1024-1050 (ConnectionError retry path): third copy of the same block
Lines 1056-1082 (ConnectionError final failure path): fourth copy — the only difference from the second copy is the logger message text
findings.suggestion: Extract the save-partial-response logic into `_save_partial_streaming_response(self, full_response, save_path, prompt, model_name, chunk_count, start_time, partial)` and call it from each exception branch with the varying arguments.

findings.summary: `LLMConfig.from_env()` is called repeatedly inside `generate_review_with_metrics` per-loop iteration rather than being passed in as a parameter
evidence: Line 527: `max_tokens = LLMConfig.from_env().long_max_tokens` — creates a config object when `max_tokens` is None

Line 583: `config = LLMConfig.from_env()` — creates another config object inside the per-attempt streaming loop to read `review_timeout`, `heartbeat_interval`, `stall_threshold`, `early_warning_threshold`
Line 493: `max_length = LLMConfig.from_env().max_input_length` inside `extract_manuscript_text`
The caller `create_review_client` (line 315) already constructs a `LLMConfig` — this config is not passed down, so it is reconstructed independently at least 3 times per review generation
findings.suggestion: Thread the `LLMConfig` instance from `create_review_client` through `generate_review_with_metrics` (and `extract_manuscript_text`) as an explicit parameter rather than reconstructing it at call sites.

findings.summary: The stall detection in `stream_query` compares `current_time - last_chunk_time` but `last_chunk_time` is set to `current_time` on the same iteration, so the condition can never be true within a single chunk
evidence: Line 870: `last_chunk_time = current_time`

Lines 873-876: `if (last_chunk_time and (current_time - last_chunk_time) > self.config.stall_threshold)` — `last_chunk_time` was just assigned `current_time`, so `current_time - last_chunk_time == 0` at this point
The stall check uses the wrong variable — it should compare against the time of the *previous* chunk, which would require saving `last_chunk_time` before updating it
findings.suggestion: Save the previous chunk time before updating: `prev_chunk_time = last_chunk_time; last_chunk_time = current_time` and use `prev_chunk_time` in the stall guard, checking `prev_chunk_time is not None` as the None sentinel.

findings.summary: The 30%-timeout warning in `stream_query` has no rate-limiting guard, so it emits a log message for every single streamed chunk after the threshold is crossed
evidence: Lines 911-924: `if elapsed > self.config.timeout * 0.3` is checked inside the per-chunk `if chunk:` block with no deduplication or interval guard
For a model generating 500 tokens past the 30% mark, this emits 500 separate `INFO` log messages with almost identical content
The condition only checks `remaining > 0`, not 'has this already been logged recently'
findings.suggestion: Track a boolean `_timeout_warned` flag (or the last-warned elapsed value) outside the chunk loop and only emit the warning the first time (or at most once per 10% of timeout remaining).

findings.summary: `clean_coverage_files` prepends `**/` to patterns that already start with `**/`, producing invalid triple-star globs for recursive patterns
evidence: Lines 339-342: `glob_pattern = f"**/{pattern}" if not pattern.startswith("**/") else pattern` — the guard `not pattern.startswith("**/")` is correct, but the default patterns on lines 327-329 ALL start with `**/`
Line 342: `for file_path in repo_root.glob(glob_pattern)` — because the guard is correct, the existing defaults are passed through unchanged, which is fine

However the branch `f"**/{pattern}"` will fire for any custom caller-supplied pattern that lacks `**/` prefix, e.g. `.coverage` → `**/.coverage`, which is correct; but a caller passing e.g. `.coverage*` would get `**/.coverage*` while the docstring says 'file patterns' without stating this transformation, creating a surprising implicit rewrite
findings.suggestion: Document the auto-prefix behavior explicitly in the docstring, or normalize all patterns to be absolute glob patterns and remove the conditional prefix logic.

findings.summary: A `pass` branch in `copy_final_deliverables` silently does nothing after an existence check, making the intent unreadable
evidence: Lines 526-532: `if combined_pdf_src.exists(): pass` — the `pass` branch documents a fallback path but actually performs no action and the comment is on the `else` not the `if`
The logic flow `if exists: pass; else: reassign` is semantically equivalent to `if not exists: reassign` but reads as if the `if` branch intentionally skips something
This pattern was likely a placeholder that was never cleaned up
findings.suggestion: Replace with `if not project_specific_pdf.exists(): combined_pdf_src, combined_pdf_dst = generic_pdf, output_dir / 'project_combined.pdf'` for a direct single-branch form.

findings.summary: `generate_review_with_metrics` combines prompt rendering, retry orchestration, streaming, heartbeat monitoring, progress display, deduplication, and quality validation in a single 140-line body
evidence: Lines 513-675 (approx): the function body contains: template render (line 537), retry loop (line 546), temperature adjustment per attempt (line 551), off-topic retry prompt construction (lines 555-569), StreamingProgress lifecycle (lines 577-608), StreamHeartbeatMonitor lifecycle (lines 584-602), fallback to non-streaming query (line 612), quality validation (lines 614-616), best-response tracking (line 619), deduplication post-processing (lines 640-648), and metrics assembly

The `except Exception: response = client.query(...)` on line 612 silently swallows the streaming failure and falls back — this control-flow branch is buried inside a 3-level nesting of loops and try blocks

The function takes 9 parameters (client, text, review_type, review_name, template_class, model_name, temperature, max_tokens, max_retries) — several of these encode policy decisions that belong in a caller-level config object
findings.suggestion: Extract at minimum: (1) `_stream_with_heartbeat(client, prompt, options, review_name, config)` for the streaming+monitoring concern; (2) `_build_retry_prompt(prompt, had_off_topic)` for prompt mutation; leaving `generate_review_with_metrics` as a retry loop coordinator.

findings.summary: `_execute_pipeline` and the latter half of `_resume_pipeline` implement parallel stage-execution loops with divergent exit-code-2 handling, creating two maintained versions of the same logic
evidence: Lines 195-202 (`_execute_pipeline`): exit code 2 handled by reconstructing a `PipelineStageResult` with `success=True`

Lines 370-379 (`_resume_pipeline`): `if hasattr(result, 'exit_code') and result.exit_code == 2` — uses `hasattr` defensively on a typed dataclass, then reconstructs an identical `PipelineStageResult` with the same fields
The `hasattr` guard on a `PipelineStageResult` (which always has `exit_code`) is a design smell indicating the two loops were written independently
Both loops also independently call `self._save_checkpoint(...)` after successful stages without sharing the logic
findings.suggestion: Extract the stage-execution, exit-code-2 normalization, and checkpoint-save into `_run_single_stage(stage_spec, executed_stage_num, pipeline_start)` returning a `PipelineStageResult`, and call it from both `_execute_pipeline` and the resume loop.

findings.summary: `validate_review_quality` encodes per-review-type structural expectations (header lists, score patterns, section variations) inline as local literals, making the function 310 lines of type-keyed conditionals
evidence: Lines 161-311: five `if/elif review_type ==` blocks each containing multi-element lists of keyword variations, regex patterns, and section names as string literals
The `header_variations` list on lines 163-177 has 5 tuples each containing a list of synonyms — this is data, not logic, but is embedded in a function body
Adding a new review type requires inserting a new `elif` branch here AND in `pipeline_runner.py`'s dispatcher AND in `REVIEW_MIN_WORDS` — no single registration point
findings.suggestion: Define a `REVIEW_TYPE_SPECS: dict[str, ReviewTypeSpec]` module-level mapping where `ReviewTypeSpec` holds expected sections, min-word overrides, and score patterns. Replace the if/elif chain with a lookup into this dict.

findings.summary: `clean_output_directories` handles log archiving, selective preservation, checkpoint preservation, subdir recreation, and root-level cleanup in a single 120-line function
evidence: Lines 97-221: the function body sequentially: (1) discovers project names via directory scan (lines 117-122), (2) calls `clean_root_output_directory` (line 125), (3) defines subdirs list (lines 127-138), (4) archives log files with timestamping (lines 151-176), (5) iterates items with checkpoint preservation logic (lines 188-211), (6) recreates subdirectory structure (lines 215-217)
Log archiving (lines 151-176) is an unrelated concern to directory cleaning and has no dedicated function

The inline project-name discovery via `projects_dir.iterdir()` (lines 117-122) duplicates logic from `infrastructure/project/discovery.py` with a comment explaining why it avoids the import (circular import)
findings.suggestion: Extract `_archive_log_files(logs_dir)` for the log-archiving concern. The circular-import workaround in the comment (lines 114-116) is a sign that `clean_output_directories` should not own project discovery — pass `project_names` as a parameter instead of computing it internally.
